title: Political Representation and Governance: Evidence from the Investment Decisions of Public Pension Funds
abstract: Representation on pension fund boards by state officials—often determined by statute decades past—is negatively related to the performance of private equity investments made by the pension fund, despite state officials’ relatively strong financial education and experience. Their underperformance appears to be partly driven by poor investment decisions consistent with political expediency, and is also positively related to political contributions from the finance industry. Boards dominated by elected rank-and-file plan participants also underperform, but to a smaller extent and due to these trustees’ lesser financial experience.

article.introduction:
How do politicians affect the governanceof the organizations with which they are associated as board members or fiduciaries? A large literature suggests that political connections can be extremely valuable for firms (Fisman (2001), Johnson and Mitton (2003), Faccio (2006), Cooper, Gulen, and Ovtchinnikov (2010), Duchin and Sosyura (2012), Amore and Bennedsen (2013), Goldman, Rocholl, and So (2013), Akey (2015), Acemoglu et al. (2016)). While existing literature focuses on cost of capital and procurement contracts as channels for political influence, an unexplored channel through which political influence may also affect governance is public asset management boards. In this paper, we demonstrate that political representation on asset management boards has very different effects from those previously demonstrated, and we shed light on mechanisms that may lead to these differences.
Our setting is the universe of U.S. public pension funds. Public pension fund boards of trustees—the composition of which is mostly fixed over time and set decades in advance—are characterized by both high average levels of political representation and considerable heterogeneity in the extent of political representation across boards. These features provide us with a laboratory for exploring whether political representation on boards affects decisions and outcomes, and, if so, whether on average politicians improve or detract from investment performance. On the one hand, politicians involved in asset management might be able to use their influence or expertise to gain access to and direct assets into higher performing investments. On the other hand, conflicts of interest or a lack of financial expertise might lead politicians to pursue political goals, at the cost of the financial returns of the investments. The latter would be similar to the behavior of overtly political investors such as sovereign wealth funds (Bernstein, Lerner, and Schoar (2013)).
The decisions we examine are the pension fund's investment allocations and performance in the private equity (PE) asset class, specifically, buyout, venture capital (VC), real estate (RE), natural resources, funds of funds, and other miscellaneous private investment categories. The PE setting is well suited to examine investment performance for a number of reasons. First, the investment policies of public pension systems have shifted markedly toward alternative investments.1Second, PE exhibits a large interquartile spread in fund performance, even within relatively narrowly defined fund types, and therefore the quality of investment selection is likely to have large consequences for performance. Third, the relative opacity of the asset class raises the potential for nonfinancial incentives to affect investment decisions. Finally, each investment has a clear investment date at which it is entered into, namely, the fund's initial closing, which is commonly referred to as the fund's vintage year. We can therefore attribute each investment decision to the board members who served in that specific year.2
There is substantial heterogeneity across public pension funds in the performance of buyout, VC, and RE investments. Prior literature examines differences in the PE performance of different types of institutional investors (Lerner, Schoar, and Wongsunwai (2007), Sensoy, Wang, and Weisbach (2014)). To the extent that there are differences, these are attributed to differences in investment objectives, incentives, or investor sophistication. In theory, however, all public pension funds should share at least one objective: to provide the benefits promised to participants as efficiently as possible for taxpayers. In our context, it is the relative representation of politicians on the board of trustees that introduces differences in incentives across pension funds. We therefore focus on the relationship between the fraction of state officials who sit on the board, and the nature and performance of the PE investments made by the pension fund.
The performance of public pension funds in PE as measured by net internal rate of return (IRR) is strongly related to the relative representation of politicians on their boards: each 10 percentage point increase in the fraction of board members who are state officials reduces net IRR by 0.9 percentage points if the official is appointed by another state official and by 0.5 percentage points if the official sits on the board by virtue of her office (ex officio), relative to the omitted category, participant-appointed board members. While other categories of trustees also exhibit performance differences, they are of smaller magnitude; for example, a 10 percentage point increase in elected plan participants on the board reduces net IRR by 0.2 to 0.4 percentage points. Appointed members of the general public do not perform better than appointed members of the pension plan itself, and relative to their financial experience may even underperform.3These results are strongest within VC and RE, and they are not explained by the pension fund's choice of investment consultants, risk-taking, or experience with PE.
Why might pension funds whose boards contain greater representation by state officials underperform? Shleifer (1996) summarizes three theoretical channels for poor policy-related decision making on the part of state officials. First, the Control channel posits that politicians may make suboptimal decisions if a desire to increase political support leads them to pursue legislation, regulation, or other political actions that advance the interests of particular industries, unions, or trade groups (see also Stigler (1971)). Second, the Corruption channel predicts that politicians may make suboptimal decisions if a desire for personal gains leads them to pursue quid pro quo, bribes, or kickbacks (see, e.g., Shleifer and Vishny (1994), Fisman, Schulz, and Vig (2014)). In our setting, this may correspond to outright bribes, future jobs in the private sector, or political contributions to the extent that such funds are used for personal gain. Finally, the Confusion channel predicts that politicians may make suboptimal decisions if a lack of knowledge, expertise, or ability leads them to use incorrect economic models or parameters when making decisions.
Applied to our setting, these channels first imply that boards with state officials who face conflicts of interest may not allocate assets to maximize financial returns. Board members receive limited remuneration. As a result, incentives to exert effort to select the best performing investments may not be sufficient to dominate incentives to invest for political gain (the Control hypothesis) or for personal gain (the Corruption hypothesis).4In addition, if state officials have less financial knowledge or expertise, boards with more state officials may underperform relative to boards with more knowledgeable trustees (the Confusion hypothesis), ceteris paribus.
To provide evidence for these hypotheses, we first control for fund types. Under the Control channel, boards with a larger fraction of state officials may be more likely to invest in PE funds that may be perceived as beneficial to the local economy. We find that controlling for fund-type categories explains around 20% to 30% of the performance differences, with this effect primarily due to pension funds with boards that have more state officials investing more heavily in RE. The remaining 70% to 80% of the performance differential is explained by differences in performance within asset classes, particularly within VC and RE. The fact that political boards underperform most within VC and RE is consistent with the Control channel, as these are the fund types that officials could arguably claim benefit the local economy.
Moreover, within fund types, we find that the share of state officials is strongly correlated with a tendency of pension funds to bias the allocation toward in-state investments using the local bias measures of Hochberg and Rauh (2013). The share of state officials is also strongly associated with other known proxies for poor investment selection in PE, which explain an additional 20% to 30% of the underperformance by board members who are state officials. These findings suggest a role for the Control channel, but also suggest that other channels may be at play.
While data availability limits our ability to explore certain mechanisms for potential quid pro quo investments, we shed some light on the Corruption channel by examining the relationship between fund performance and political contributions to the campaigns of elected officials during their tenure as board members. We find that political contributions from the finance industry help explain part of the negative performance effect of government officials on boards, which suggests that at least some of the underperformance may be related to political incentives. To the extent that politicians derive personal gains from contributions, this evidence suggests that the Corruption channel also plays a role.
Finally, to test the Confusion channel, we examine the extent to which the performance differences are driven by differences in financial expertise across types of board members. We find that the weaker financial experience of elected plan participants explains most or all of their underperformance. It does not, however, explain the performance of state appointed and ex officio members who on average score moderately well on financial expertise but display the largest underperformance of the trustees in the sample. The Confusion channel is therefore unlikely to be driving the underperformance of pension fund boards with a high representation of state officials, although it does explain the underperformance of boards with a large share of elected plan participants.
In sum, our findings suggest a role for two out of the three theoretically posited channels for poor decision making on the part of political officials: political incentives to favor state-related investments, and personal incentives to engage in quid pro quo investments. We note, however, that our findings should be viewed as providing suggestive support for a role for these two channels, as it is difficult to disentangle the two channels, and some of our findings may be interpreted as support for either channel. In contrast, our findings indicate that the Confusion channel cannot explain the underperformance of political boards.
An important question that arises is whether the board structures we study have emerged endogenously, perhaps as a result of the styles and outcomes of pension funds’ previous investments. The fact that regulations pertaining to the board composition of most pension funds were adopted long ago, before PE became an important asset class,5reduces concerns regarding reverse causality. Nonetheless, we show that the results are robust to excluding pension funds with changes in board structure during the sample period and to the inclusion of fixed effects for pension fund state, so that performance differences emerge between pension funds located in the same state that have differences in board composition in their charters. We also show that the underperformance of political boards also manifests itself in specifications that include pension fund fixed effects, which exploit only the variation from the 17.5% of the pension fund sample that experiences changes in board composition.
Our paper contributes to several studies. First, as noted above, we contribute to the literature on the effect of political influence on boards, decisions, and outcomes. This literature generally concludes that political connections are value-increasing. However, the ability of politicians to advance their own interests using public money implies substantial potential costs, rather than benefits, of political connections. Our setting allows us to examine whether the presence of politicians is actually beneficial to taxpayers and pension beneficiaries (the public sector analog of shareholders and employees, respectively, in the corporate context). We find that politicians, likely due to control and/or corruption motivations, invest public pension fund assets in PE funds that deliver lower returns and the costs may be paid for to a large extent by plan participants and taxpayers.
Second, our paper contributes to the literature on public pension underfunding and investment incentives under current accounting and regulatory regimes (Novy-Marx and Rauh (2011), Andonov, Bauer, and Cremers (2017)), and on the relation between characteristics of pension fund boards and overall fund performance and asset allocations (Mitchell and Hsin (1999), Useem and Mitchell (2000), Coronado, Engen, and Knight (2003), Mitchell and Yang (2008), Bradley, Pantzalis, and Yuan (2016)). Public pension funds are important to both sponsoring governments and their employees, as large amounts of taxpayer and public employee money are at stake.6Previous research suggests that pension funds do not always pursue pure value maximization (Del Guercio and Hawkins (1999), Agrawal (2012), Barber, Morse, and Yasuda (2016)). We extend this work by showing that public pension funds governed by politicians are relatively more susceptible to pursuing political or personal gains at the expense of financial performance.
Third, we contribute to the asset management literature on the relationship between investment performance and characteristics of the decision makers, typically mutual fund managers or individual investors (Chevalier and Ellison (1999), Korniotis and Kumar (2011)), by studying the importance of prior professional experience and education in the context of pension fund boards. Finally, we add to the literature on the drivers of differences in PE performance across types of limited partners (LPs; Lerner, Schoar, and Wongsunwai (2007), Hochberg and Rauh (2013), Sensoy, Wang, and Weisbach (2014)) by documenting differences in performance within public pension funds, the most important type of LP based on the number and size of investments.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we provide an overview of pension fund governance and the role of boards. SectionIIdescribes the data and sample. SectionIIIprovides results on board composition and PE performance. In SectionIV, we test the Control hypothesis and examine investment selection. In SectionV, we focus on the Corruption channel and analyze the relation between political contributions and performance. In SectionVI, we test the Confusion hypothesis and examine the relation between experience, skills, and performance. Finally in SectionVIIwe conclude.
article.I. Overview of Pension Fund Governance a:
The board of administration for a public pension fund is responsible for the management and control of the pension fund. For example, the CalPERS Board has exclusive control of the administration and investment of funds. The board's responsibilities include setting employer contribution rates, determining investment asset allocations, and providing actuarial valuations, among other things. Similarly, the Teacher Retirement System (TRS) of Texas was established pursuant to Article 16, Section 67 of the Texas Constitution, which requires that Texas TRS has a Board of Trustees to administer the TRS and invest its assets. We focus on pension fund boards, as their members have the power and responsibility to make investment decisions on behalf of the fund.
If a pension fund has a separate board that makes investment decisions, we analyze the composition of this investment board. For example, the assets of multiple pension funds from the State of Washington, such as Washington PERS 1/2/3, LEOFF 1/2, School Employees 2/3, and Teachers 1/2/3, are pooled together and managed by Washington State Investment Board (SIB).7In our analysis, we collect data on Washington SIB trustees who are responsible for investment decisions. If the separate investment committee only makes recommendations, we collect the composition of the main board that votes on and approves investments.8
The compensation of pension fund board members differs substantially from the compensation received by directors of corporations. For instance, the board members of the State Teachers Retirement System of Ohio serve without compensation other than actual, necessary expenses. Similarly, board members of Washington SIB who are public employees serve without compensation, while board members who are not public employees are compensated in accordance with a state statute (RCW 43.03.240) that in 2016 paid $50 per day.
We classify board members into three overarching categories: state, public, and participant. State board members are government officials of the state, county, city, or other public entity; participant board members are currently employed or retired plan participants; public trustees are members of the general public who do not work for the state or participate in the pension plan. We further classify board members by the mechanism through which they obtain their board seat: appointed by a government executive, serving by virtue of holding another position (ex officio), or elected by plan members. We thus have a total of nine categories of pension fund board members:state-appointed,state-ex officio,state-elected,participant-appointed, participant-ex officio,participant-elected,public-appointed,public-ex officio, andpublic-elected. Of these nine categories, five represent the vast majority of all pension fund trustees: state-appointed, state-exofficio, participant-appointed, participant-elected, and public-appointed. Overall, we observe a great deal of heterogeneity in board composition across pension funds.
Typical examples of state-ex officio board members include state treasurer, controller, personnel director, director of finance, and superintendent. State-appointed trustees are usually appointed by the governor, mayor, speaker of the state house of representatives, or president of the state senate; frequent examples are senators, representatives, elected officials of local government, and school board representatives. State-elected board members participate on the boards of only four funds in our sample. They are also state officials, but they are elected by plan participants.9
Almost all pension funds have trustees representing plan participants, but their proportion and appointment procedure varies across funds. The majority of these trustees are either elected by plan participants or appointed to the board. Additionally, nine pension funds in our sample have ex officio participant board members who are usually (but not always) union representatives.10In the case of elections, active and retired participants either vote at the annual meeting or receive ballots by post. The appointment procedure of trustees representing plan participants involves two groups of stakeholders. Typically, plan participants nominate several candidates and a state official appoints one of them to the pension fund board. For instance, in the Texas Teachers Retirement System, one board member is appointed by the governor from the three higher education candidates who have been nominated by employees of higher education institutions.
General public board members typically work in the local financial industry and are appointed to the board by state officials. In our sample, only four pension funds have general public board members who are elected by plan participants, and one fund has general public ex officio board members.11In all other funds these trustees are appointed to the board. For example, the California State Teachers Retirement System (CalSTRS) has three general public board members appointed by the governor and confirmed by the senate. In 2014, these trustees worked at an investment banking firm, VC firm, and insurance company, respectively.12
State trustees are expected to have a moderate level of financial sophistication, with some (e.g., those who serve as state treasurer) having extensive knowledge. State officials may also have social and political connections that could benefit the pension funds on whose boards they sit, particularly in the realm of access to PE investment opportunities, which are not always open to any investor and are strictly controlled by PE fund managers. However, state trustees might have incentives to overweight those asset management firms that contribute money to their political party or otherwise lend support to their personal career prospects. On the one hand, their incentives to improve pension fund performance should be strong, as the better the investment performance, the fewer resources taxpayers will need to devote to pension funding. On the other hand, states can exercise substantial discretion in their application of government accounting to postpone contributions that would be necessary to fund pension liabilities (Novy-Marx and Rauh (2014)).
Other types of pension fund trustees, such as plan participants and members of the public, may have different skills and incentives. Plan participants are expected to have the least financial experience, as their careers are in teaching, public safety, or another area of public service. However, conflicts of interest are likely low for this group, as their connections to the financial industry are presumably minimal. They may or may not have an incentive to exert effort and care about fund performance, depending on whether they view taxpayers or beneficiaries as residual claimants for funding surpluses or shortfalls (Novy-Marx and Rauh (2009)). On the one hand, in many states strong legal provisions protect pension benefits from being reduced, which suggests that participants are at least partially insulated from the effects of poor investment performance.13On the other hand, legislatures in many states have increased pension benefits following periods of high asset returns.14Furthermore, in states with weaker legal protections of pension benefits, some governments have implemented reforms such as reductions in cost of living adjustments and increases in required employee contributions.
General public trustees are expected to have the most financial experience, as they are often chosen to the board based on their expertise. However, the fact that they are finance practitioners may give rise to potential opportunistic behavior. Some pension funds place explicit restrictions on public trustees. For instance, San Diego County Employee Retirement System (ERS) stipulates that trustees “must not have any personal interests which would create a conflict of interest with the duties of a board member.” Public trustees receive no direct benefits from pension funding, but presumably would prefer to avoid increases in the tax dollars devoted to pension funding if investment returns can serve as a partial replacement (Novy-Marx and Rauh (2011,2014)).
If we observe substantial differences in performance based on politicians’ share of the board of trustees, an important question that arises is whether these trustees affect investment decisions and performance, or whether the extent of political representation on these boards emerged endogenously as a result of the styles and outcomes of the pension funds’ prior investments. For all pension funds in our sample, we collect the year when they were established. For some funds, particularly the largest, we also collect the date when state laws defined the current board composition. Using this information, for each pension fund in our sample we check whether board composition changed during the sample period.
We find that there is a great deal of stability in board structures, and that most regulations pertaining to funds’ board composition were adopted long ago. For example, the board composition of county pension funds in California (Los Angeles County ERS, Orange County ERS, etc.) was established by the County Employees Retirement Law of 1937 and has not changed since at least 1947. Among large funds, the composition of the New York State Teachers Retirement System board has not changed since at least 1976, despite two changes in the election process for participant-elected trustees,15and the composition of the Florida State Board of Administration has not changed since it was established in 1970.16Importantly, the composition of most of the boards we analyze was set decades before PE became an established asset class and before ERISA clarifications made investment in PE allowable for pension funds.17, 18
The time-series variation in board composition is rather limited, with only 17.5% of the public pension funds in the sample experiencing a change in overall board composition during our sample period. Furthermore, in cases in which there were board changes, such changes may have been prompted by poor investment performance, which raises the concern that pension funds with board changes contaminate the results through reverse causality. We check the robustness of the results to excluding pension funds with a change in board composition and find that the results remain strongly significant. Since board structures (and therefore relative political representation) were generally established long before PE became an important part of investor portfolios, reverse causality is not likely to explain our results.
article.II. Data and Sample:
Our data come from four primary sources. First, we collect data on public pension funds’ board composition from their Comprehensive Annual Financial Reports (CAFRs), which report board composition in the Introduction section and clarify the exact regulation in the Financial section of the report. We also examine state or municipal codes and statues to verify the board composition and to obtain information on the election and appointment procedures.19
Second, we collect biographical information on trustees from a generalized web search. Specifically, we collect information on each trustee who served on the board of one of the largest 46 public pension funds in our sample. These funds represent around 13% (30%) of the assets managed by U.S. pension funds (U.S.-defined benefit pension funds) in 2011,20and around three-quarters of the PE investments; Internet Appendix Table IAIII lists these 46 pension funds. The information on trustees includes educational background, union membership, executive experience, and other relevant prior experience. We classify trustees as having asset management experience if they have prior experience in asset management, pension fund investment management (not administration), hedge funds, PE, commercial RE, or VC. We classify trustees as having financial experience if they have prior experience in banking, risk management, or insurance, if they have served as CEO/CFO/CIO in a large corporation, or if they have practiced financial law (cases in M&A, bond issuance, commercial RE, securitization). Related experience corresponds to prior public finance (treasurer, budget analyst, head of budget committee), actuarial, employee benefit management, or credit union management experience. Finally, we classify trustees as having director experience if they have held an executive position in the private sector or managed their own business, and as having private sector experience if they have a private sector experience more generally.
Third, for the board members who were candidates in a local, state, or federal political election during their board service, we compute the political contributions received by the trustee during the last election cycle, and we determine whether these candidates received political donations from financial industry-related institutions during their election campaign. We collect these data from the Follow the Money (www.followthemoney.org) database of the National Institute on Money in State Politics.21
Finally, we obtain data on PE investments from Preqin. The vast majority of institutional investments in PE are made via legally separate funds run by professional managers (referred to as GPs), as the selection of appropriate direct investments requires more resources and specialized human capital that few institutional investors have (Fang, Ivashina, and Lerner (2015)). PE funds are raised for a specified period (typically 10 to 12 years, with possible short extensions) and are governed by partnership agreements between the investors and the fund's principals. Investments are made by the LPs at the start of the fund's life, often referred to as the vintage year. Using the vintage year, we can attribute each investment decision to the pension fund board members who served on the board in that year.
We collect the investments made by public pension funds into PE funds raised in vintage years 1990 to 2011. Our sample contains 13,559 investments by 212 unique public pension fund LPs investing in 3,959 PE funds managed by 1,430 GPs. Table1presents summary statistics for pension fund board composition and investments.22The pension funds in our sample have an average of $43.38 billion in assets under management (AUM) and 9.3 board members. Panel A of Table1presents summary statistics for the pension funds and investments that have at least one board member who can be classified in one of the nine categories of board trustees. On average, state-appointed and state-exofficio trustees represent around 7.6% and 25.4% of our sample board members. Trustees who represent plan participants are present in 199 of the 212 sample pension funds and hold on average 40.2% of the board positions. The average proportion of participant-elected board members is 27.0%, whereas plan members appointed to the board account for 11.5%. General public board members hold, on average, 25.5% of the sample board seats, and almost all of them are appointed. Figure1presents a pie chart summarizing average board composition weighted by number of PE investments. Figure2provides histograms of board composition and shows that there is significant cross-sectional dispersion in the representation of the different categories of pension fund board members.
Panel B presents summary statistics for the key performance measures—net IRR and multiple of invested capital—for the subsamples of the 13,559 PE investments for which these data items are available. The mean fund in our sample has a net IRR of 10.40%, a net multiple of invested capital of 1.44, and a total committed capital base of $2.24 billion. The median (average) PE fund in our sample has a total of 17 (26.2) LPs, and a sequence number of 3 (3.96). The median (average) investment size by a public pension fund in a PE fund is $30 ($59.86) million.23
Cash flow data are available only for a subset of the PE funds in our sample. The benefit of cash flow data is that they allow us to calculate a variety of public market equivalent (PME) return statistics. Internet Appendix Table IA.XXI presents results of logit regressions in which the dependent variable is equal to 1 if cash flow data are available in Preqin for a given fund. The results document a selection bias in the availability of the cash flow data. In particular, underperforming pension funds governed by boards with a large proportion of politicians often have investments with missing cash flows. Cash flows are more likely to be missing for PE funds with worse performance, and are especially underpopulated for RE and funds of funds. That said, in Internet Appendix Section IV we replicate all the tests in the paper using PMEs for the subsample for which cash flow data are available as a performance measure, and we find that all key results continue to go through.
In Panel C of Table1, we split PE investments into six fund types. Pension funds allocate approximately 36% of the number of investments to buyout funds, 20% to VC, 21% to RE, 3% to natural resources, 7% to funds of funds, and 12% to other funds. Other funds capture investments in distressed debt, secondaries, coinvestments, hybrid, and balanced funds. The allocation across fund types is similar when we use the dollar amount of committed capital instead of the number of investments. The only difference is that the relative weight of VC investments is lower when using commitment data because VC funds are on average smaller. We report the statistics using the number of investments because the commitment data are not available for 15% of the sample investments (typically made by smaller pension funds).
Panel A of Table2presents summary statistics on the skills and professional experience of pension fund board members who served during the 1990 to 2011 period. We match the background data collected on the trustees of the 46 largest pension funds to the 9,492 investments made by these funds.24When presenting summary statistics for trustees, we assign an equal weight to each trustee in the sample. Of the 1,185 unique trustees, 20.3% have experience in asset management, 14.4% have experience in finance more generally, and 36.6% have other related experience. Moreover, 2.4% of board members hold a CFA (Chartered Financial Analyst); 12.7% have an MBA degree; and 37.4% have a bachelors, masters, or PhD degree in finance, economics, business management, business administration, accounting, or insurance. Figure3depicts the percentage of trustees with relevant experience across the five main categories of trustees.
A few patterns emerge from the statistics in Table2, as is apparent in Figure3. Public-appointed board trustees appear to have more relevant experience than the other groups, followed by state political trustees: over 42% of public-appointed trustees have experience in asset management, 30% have other general financial experience, and 23% have other related experience. Participant-appointed trustees tend to have more relevant experience, and more executive experience in the private sector, than participant-elected trustees. Participant-elected trustees are more likely to be union members, as unions typically nominate or recommend candidates during trustee elections. Public-appointed members are more likely to have obtained a relevant educational degree or an MBA.
Panel B of Table2reports summary statistics on political engagement and contributions. State trustees are more likely to participate in political elections (many of them become trustees after winning elections that automatically make them ex officio trustees). For the trustees who participate in local, state, or federal political electionsduringtheir tenure as a pension fund board member, we collect data on political contributions received by their campaign. Thus, the existence of contributions data is conditional on matching the tenure of a board member to simultaneous involvement in politics.25We calculate both the total amount of contributions and contributions from the financial industry in particular. In general, state-ex officio trustees receive more total contributions and more contributions from the finance industry than other trustee candidates.
article.III. Political Representatives on Boards:
We begin our empirical analysis in Table3with a regression of investment performance on board composition. The observation level is the LP-investment. In models (1) to (3), we measure performance using net IRR, whereas in models (4) to (6) we measure performance using multiple of invested capital. As independent variables, we use the percentage of trustees that fall into four of the five large categories of board members (state-appointed, state-ex officio, participant-elected, and public-appointed) for the pension fund board in the year of the observation. The omitted category is participant-appointed. We control for the natural logarithm of the LP's AUM, board size, and the natural logarithm of the commitment as a percentage of the AUM. In each model, we include vintage year fixed effects and independently double-cluster standard errors by pension fund and vintage (Petersen (2009)).26In models (2), (3), (5), and (6), we include LP state fixed effects to capture state-specific factors that are relatively constant over time, like union power or importance of the financial industry. The number of observations in columns that include the control for the commitment amount is lower as some investments do not have this information.
A clear pattern can be seen from the estimates in the table. Recalling that the omitted category is participant-appointed, pension funds with boards that have higher percentages of state-appointed, state-ex officio, or participant-elected trustees exhibit consistently lower performance in terms of both net IRR and multiple of invested capital. While public-appointed members have negative coefficients in some specifications, there is not a robust statistical difference between their performance and that of the omitted category (participant-appointed) after controlling for commitment size and LP state fixed effects. Specifically, focusing first on the net IRR measure of performance, we find that of the three underperforming categories, state-appointed board members are associated with the lowest performance: a 10 percentage point increase in the proportion of the board that consists of state-appointed members is associated with a roughly 0.91 percentage point decrease in annual net IRR. State-ex officio board members have the next lowest performance: a 10 percentage point increase in the proportion of the board that consists of state-ex officio members is associated with a 0.52 to 0.68 percentage point decrease in annual net IRR. The effects for higher levels of participant-elected board trustees are lower, but remain statistically significant across specifications: a 10 percentage point increase in the proportion of the board that consists of participant-elected members is associated with a 0.20 to 0.42 percentage point decrease in annual net IRR.
When we turn to the multiple of invested capital performance measure, we find that pension funds governed by 10 percentage points more state-appointed trustees select PE investments that underperform by 26% to 44% of invested capital. A 10 percentage point increase in the proportion of the board that consists of state-ex officio members is associated with a 16% to 27% decrease in multiple of invested capital. We also find that participant-elected trustees invest in funds that deliver lower multiples.27
Next, we analyze whether board composition indeed affects investments and performance. Internet Appendix Table IA.V lists the 37 funds that change their board composition over the sample period as well as the year when their board composition changed. While it may be useful to study the response of investment returns to these changes, board composition is perhaps more likely to be endogenous to pension fund performance in these instances. In Table4we therefore analyze two subsamples separately: (i) the subsample of pension funds that donotchange their board composition during the sample period, in which variation in the explanatory variables cannot by definition be caused by PE performance, and (ii) the subsample of pension funds thatdochange their board composition during the sample period, in which the analysis can include pension fund (LP) fixed effects to control for unobserved heterogeneity in LPs.
We run three sets of tests in Table4. In columns (1) and (5), we examine the performance of 175 pension funds that have not changed their board composition during their presence in the data. In columns (2) and (6), we run a stricter test and examine only those pension funds that have not changed their board composition at least since 1985, even if they did not begin investing in PE until much later. We track legislative amendments to the statutes defining board composition and identify 47 pension funds that maintained the same board structure since 1985.28In columns (3), (4), (7), and (8), we analyze the subsample of pension funds thatdidchange their board structures over the sample period. Nearly all the changes remove state officials from the board and replace them with public-appointed members.29In these models, we include pension fund (LP) fixed effects, such that we identify our estimates of the changes in pension fund board composition. In columns (3) and (7) we run regressions in which we pool both main types of state officials into one category, and in columns (4) and (8) we separate the categories as in the main models. Since nearly all of the changes involve one-to-one replacement of politicians with public-appointed members, and since our models contain LP fixed effects, we exclude the public-appointed variable in these columns.
Overall, the results in Table4confirm that boards with higher fractions of state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected trustees exhibit lower performance, with magnitudes similar to those exhibited in the full sample. This is true both when we exclude boards with a change in composition and when we restrict attention to boards that had a change in composition and add LP fixed effects. Limiting the sample to boards with no change addresses concerns about reverse causality: if board composition does not change, this means that it does not respond to poor performance. All three sets of models in Table4support the interpretation that board composition leads to lower performance, rather than vice versa.
In Table5, we conduct a number of additional robustness checks. In this table, we use the net IRR performance measure. In Internet Appendix Table IA.XII, we present results of the same regressions but using the multiple of invested capital performance measure. First, to address the concern that investment selection is driven by consultant choices rather than by choices made by the board itself, in column (1) we collect data on investment consultants for 206 pension funds and include fixed effects for the 29 identified consultant groups and a dummy for having no consultant.30We also control for differences in organizational structure by including a dummy variable equal to 1 if a pension fund has a separate investment board that only makes asset allocation and investment decisions and another board that is responsible for other administrative aspects of the retirement system, like managing the retiree healthcare program, processing disability applications, and so on. The majority of pension funds use one board and do not separate responsibilities. Second, to address the concern that poor performance is due to a lack of experience or late entry into the PE asset class, in column (2) we add a control for the LP's prior experience in PE and the year of the LP's first investment in PE. We estimate an LP's experience in PE as one minus the ratio of the number of PE investments made in the current year relative to the total number of investments made in the current year and in the previous four years. We use five-year rolling windows, as the median tenure of pension fund board members is 5.3 years. A lower value of this measure indicates lower prior experience in PE.
As can be seen in Table5, in both models we obtain estimates qualitatively similar to those in our main models in terms of both economic and statistical significance. In particular, higher percentages of state-appointed and state-ex officio board members are associated with lower net IRR. Moreover, as expected, low prior experience in PE investing is associated with lower returns: a one standard deviation increase in the PE experience measure that we construct is associated with a 0.88 percentage point worse performance.
A third concern is that the differences in performance may be driven by differences in access to better performing PE funds. In column (3), we restrict the sample of investments to the first PE fund raised by a GP, as first-time funds rarely ration access (Sensoy, Wang, and Weisbach (2014), Cavagnaro et al. (2017)). In column (4), we limit attention to the subsample of LPs with below-median AUM, as smaller LPs may be more likely to have trouble accessing brand-name PE funds. Once again, we observe patterns similar to those in our main models, although economic magnitudes here are larger. In particular, a 10 percentage point increase in the percentage of state-appointed board members is associated with a 1.53 percentage point lower net IRR, and a 10 percentage point increase in the percentage of state-ex officio board members is associated with a 1.05 percentage point lower net IRR. This increase in the magnitude of the effect could be due to greater agency conflicts in smaller pension funds, or to smaller pension funds lacking bargaining power when investing in PE funds.31
A final concern that we address is that poor performance may be driven by economically targeted investment (ETI) mandates. ETIs allow pension funds under ERISA to make investments not only for the purpose of financial returns, but also to support local economies and jobs, if such investment furthers the long-term interests of their plan participants. We obtain data on ETIs from the catalog maintained by Pacific Community Ventures.32We are able to identify 160 ETI investments by public pension funds in our sample.33In Internet Appendix Table IA.XIII, we find that boards with higher political representation are indeed more likely to invest in ETIs. To determine whether this is what drives the lower performance of such boards, in column (5) we exclude all ETI observations from the regression. In column (6), we exclude both ETI funds and in-state funds, which may represent economic development-driven investments.34
In both columns, we again observe estimates that are similar in magnitude and statistical significance to those in Table3. Moreover, in Internet Appendix Table IA.XIII, we reestimate our main models with a control for whether the investment is an ETI. ETIs have 6 percentage points lower net IRR, but this does not attenuate our main result. In sum, while ETI mandates may represent one aspect of underperformance in PE investing, they do not appear to be the primary driver behind our findings. Rather, politicians appear to be using theirdiscretionto invest in poorly performing funds. While we cannot rule out the possibility that ETIs have positive externalities, it is doubtful that the social benefits to purely discretionary PE investing by politicians are large.
A natural question that arises in an asset pricing context is whether the lower returns we observe for highly political boards are perhaps a result of their investing in less risky funds, which on average would be expected to have lower returns. Due to the nature of PE, it is difficult to obtain an exact ex ante measure of risk for PE investments.35That said, there are several reasons why the results are unlikely to be driven by differences in risk.
First, a number of indicators suggest that RE funds covered by Preqin are among the riskiest categories of PE. Preqin data contain mainly opportunistic and value-added RE funds and almost no core RE funds. The fact that boards heavily populated by state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected members overweight RE is not consistent with the idea that they are investing in less risky funds and expecting lower returns for that reason. Second, Andonov, Bauer, and Cremers (2017) and Bradley, Pantzalis, and Yuan (2016) find that pension funds governed by boards populated with more state-appointed and state-ex officio board members invest more in risky asset classes such as equity and alternatives.
Third, similar to Lerner, Schoar, and Wongsunwai (2007) and Lopez-de-Silanes, Phalippou, and Gottschalg (2015), we analyze the distribution of returns. Figure4presents evidence that resembles a value-at-risk analysis and suggests that risk cannot explain the poor performance we observe in Table3. Observations here are at the LP-investment level and we present the distribution of returns for the five main categories of board members. We measure performance using net IRR minus the mean within cells of (vintage × fund type). In Internet Appendix Table IA.XIV, we present the distribution of returns based on the multiple of invested capital minus the mean value within (vintage × fund type) groups. When calculating the group means, we split the investments into the following fund types: RE, natural resources, buyout, VC, funds of funds, and other PE funds.
Board composition and performance distribution. This figure presents the distribution of returns for the five main categories of board members and resembles a value-at-risk analysis. Observations are at the LP-investment level. Performance is measured using net IRR minus the (vintage × fund type) group mean. When calculating group means, we split investments into the following fund types: buyout, VC, RE, natural resources, funds of funds, and other PE funds. For each category of board members, we split observations into three groups. The first group includes investments made by a board that has no members belonging to that category. The second group includes investments made by a board that has a below-median percentage of members belonging to that category. Finally, the third group includes investments made by a board that has an above-median percentage of board members belonging to that category. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
If participant-appointed and public-appointed trustees obtain higher returns by taking on riskier investments, they should have a higher likelihood of having PE funds in the lowest performance percentiles. In fact, their better performance comes with more limited downside. When examining the 5thand 10thpercentiles, we see that participant-appointed and public-appointed trustees avoid selecting the poorest funds, and the worse-performing trustee categories have worse performance in these percentiles. For example, the 5thpercentile for plans with no state-ex officio members is −15.67 net IRR points, and for plans with an above-median percentage of state-ex officio members it is −21.47 net IRR points. On the right side of the distribution, their performance is closer to the average performance. This suggests that it is not the case that state trustees are simply picking less-risky PE funds.36
article.IV. Political Representation and Investm:
What, then, drives the underperformance of boards characterized by high representation by state-ex officio or state-appointed trustees? Following the three theoretical channels posited by Shleifer (1996), we hypothesize that variation in risk-adjusted investment performance associated with state trustees might be driven by multiple factors. First, politicians may direct capital into investments that increase their political support or that may be viewed as beneficial to the state (the Control channel). Second, state trustees, particularly elected officials, may direct capital into investments in return for quid pro quo, bribes, or kickbacks, including in the form of political donations to their campaigns (the Corruption channel). Finally, poor investment choices may simply reflect a lack of knowledge, expertise, or experience on the part of state trustees (the Confusion channel).
Performance differentials could be due to different allocations across PE fund types or to the selection of managers within PE fund types. In testing the above hypotheses, we focus primarily on manager selection within fund types, as there are many possible determinants of a pension fund's decision to invest in different PE categories. As a preliminary step, however, we examine the relationship between board composition and asset allocation in Internet Appendix Table IA.XVI. We expect that, under the Control channel, boards with a larger fraction of state officials may allocate disproportionately more into PE fund types that are arguably related to economic development, such as RE or VC. We find supportive evidence for RE, but we observe no overweighting of VC funds.
Table6analyzes performance within fund types. Here, the dependent variable is net IRR.37We start by analyzing performance in all investments together (All), controlling for the allocation to different fund types using indicator variables. The omitted category in Table6, columns (1) and (2) is buyout funds—we include indicators for VC, RE, natural resources, funds of funds, and other PE fund types. We find that, even after controlling for differences in allocations, state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected trustees invest in PE funds that deliver lower returns. When we compare the coefficients reported in Table6, columns (1) and (2) with the coefficients reported in Table3, columns (1) and (2), we conclude that poor asset allocation decisions explain 20% to 30% of the performance differential. The remainder can be potentially attributed to poor selection of PE funds.38
Next, we turn to manager selection within asset classes. In columns (3) to (5), we analyze performance within the three main PE fund types—buyout, VC, and RE, respectively. In column (6), we combine the remaining fund types (natural resources, funds of funds, and other funds), as the number of investments in these fund types is too small to analyze them separately. We find that state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected trustees underperform within RE, VC, and other PE funds. The only exception is within buyout funds.
Overall, Table6documents that state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected exhibit both worse asset allocation and worse selection of managers within asset categories. While differences in allocation to specific categories could be driven by variation in objectives or beliefs about the properties of different asset classes, the fact that 70% to 80% of the variation remains after controlling for fund type (asset class) suggests a role for either incentives or skill in determining the choice of investments. The fact that political boards underperform most within RE and VC is consistent with the Control channel, as these are the categories that officials could arguably claim to benefit the local economy.
We hypothesize further that, under the Control mechanism, boards with a larger fractions of state officials are more likely to direct investments into “local” funds—those based in the same state as the LP (Hochberg and Rauh (2013)). Table7presents regressions in which the dependent variable is the LP'sexcess shareof in-state investments as defined by Hochberg and Rauh (2013).39The analysis is at the LP-vintage year level and we examine overweighting in all investments as well as separately within fund types.
Table7shows that state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected board members overweight in-state investments even after controlling for differences in percentage allocation to PE fund types. In column (2), we document that a 10 percentage point increase in the proportion of state-ex officio members is associated with a 1.41 percentage point higher allocation to local investments. Based on the economic magnitudes, the overweighting of local investments is even larger among state-appointed trustees.
Columns (3) to (5) of Table7present the results separately for investments in buyout, VC, and RE funds, respectively. In column (6), we combine the remaining funds (natural resources, funds of funds, and other funds) as before. We document that the in-state overweighting by state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected board members is particularly strong for RE, but is insignificant for buyout funds. For VC, the coefficients are marginally significant but the magnitudes indicate large overweighting. We do not observe significant overweighting for the smaller fund types as funds of funds are typically located in the financial centers and PE funds that invest in natural resources are often based close to these resources. Overall, the estimates in Table7suggest that the overweighting of local investments potentially explains part of the underperformance by state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected trustees, given the finding in Hochberg and Rauh (2013) that public pension funds’ in-state investments achieve lower performance than that on their similar out-of-state investments. Combined with our findings that boards with higher political representation are also more likely to invest in ETI funds, the results in Table7lend further support to the Control channel as a source of underperformance.
How else do the investment choices of differently composed boards differ? Table8presents results of regressions in which the dependent variables capture three investment characteristics. First, in columns (1) and (2), the dependent variable measures the total number of LP investors in the PE fund. Second, in columns (3) and (4), the dependent variable is the natural logarithm of the PE fund size in which the LP invested. Third, in columns (5) and (6), the dependent variable is the sequence number of the PE fund. We choose these three characteristics because they are generally associated with poorer performance in PE. Generally speaking, larger PE funds tend to exhibit higher performance given that the ability to raise large sums of money is likely to be positively correlated with the perceived quality of the PE fund manager. Managers of funds with a low sequence number are relatively new and have not yet proven their ability to perform, as would be the case with managers in higher sequence funds, which will be raised only if the manager returns good performance on her earlier sequenced funds (Kaplan and Schoar (2005), Phalippou and Gottschalg (2009)).40Finally, a low number of LPs suggests that the manager may not have been able to secure broad support for her fundraising efforts.
Based on the results in Table7, in Table8we additionally introduce the control variablesIn-state REandIn-state VC, indicators equal to 1 if the general partner of a RE or VC fund is located in the same state as the pension fund. The number of observations is lower in columns (2), (4), and (6) because we control for the relative commitment amount. We find that state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected trustees invest in smaller funds, funds with fewer investors, and funds with a lower sequence number. The number of investors counts not only the number of public pension funds acting as LP, but also the number of other LPs, like private pension funds, endowments, and foundations. Similarly, PE fund size is a sum of the commitments from all investors. We therefore document that public pension funds governed by more political boards invest more in PE funds that were shunned by other public pension funds and institutional investors.
In columns (5) and (6), we focus on the PE fund sequence number, which proxies for the experience of the GP. Our results indicate that state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected board members exhibit substantial bias toward inexperienced GPs. This effect is particularly pronounced for state-appointed trustees, where a 10 percentage point increase in their proportion is related to investing in PE funds with a 0.28 to 0.38 lower sequence number. Importantly, in Table8we control for local in-state investments as well as indicators for different fund types. We thus document an additional channel through which the investments made by state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected trustees differ from other pension funds—these trustees select poorly performing managers even when investing outside of their own state and the differences are not due to overweighting some PE fund types.41
How much of the poor performance of state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected trustees within fund types that we find in Table6can be explained by controlling for the overweighting of in-state investments in RE and VC and the selection of small funds with fewer investors and a lower sequence number? In other words, how much does overweighting of in-state investments and selection of small PE funds with few investors and low sequence numbers contribute to underperformance above and beyond the 20% to 30% of the performance differential that is explained by allocation to worse-performing fund types such as RE and funds of funds that we calculated in Table6?
The dependent variable in Table9is again the performance of public pension funds. The estimates can be compared to Table3as well as Table6, columns (1) and (2), and we now include bothIn-stateindicators and investment characteristics uncovered in Table8as additional controls. Note that we cannot include PEFund sizeand#Investorsin the same regression, as these two variables are highly correlated. The number of observations is lower in columns (3), (4), (7), and (8) becauseFund sizeis missing for some investments.
We find that these investment characteristics are significantly negatively related to performance and explain part of the underperformance by state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected board members. First, similar to Table6, we observe that investing in RE and funds of funds is negatively related to performance. Investments in local VC funds also deliver lower returns. Moreover, as expected, the variables#InvestorsandFund sizesuggest that investments selected by multiple LPs perform better. For instance, based on column (3), a one-unit increase in the log of PE fund size (a doubling of fund size) is associated with a 1.034 percentage point higher net IRR.
Importantly, after controlling for all these variables, we continue to observe significant underperformance by pension fund boards heavily populated by state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected trustees. Combined with the controls for fund type, the in-state and quality of investment selection measures explain 50% to 60% of the performance differential. In particular, the coefficient on state-appointed board members decreases from −9.113 in Table3to −6.261 in Table6(controls for fund types/allocation differences) and to −3.740 in Table9(additional controls for in-state investments and investment characteristics of funds shunned by other LPs). We demonstrate this graphically in Figure5, which compares coefficient estimates for the baseline models reported in Tables3,6, and9.
A causal relationship between political representation and poor performance would imply the policy conclusion that a given board could improve performance by replacing state officials with more independent board members from either the public or plan participants. For the results to be driven by omitted variables that are correlated with both board structure and performance, it would have to be the case that, in pension funds with many political board members, the members of the general public or plan participants who would replace them would also underperform, which could be the case if the pools of possible appointees differ in quality by state. In the absence of natural experiments in board composition, we conduct a falsification exercise. We examine whether corporate pension funds, foundations, and endowments located in states with a large number of political trustees on public pension fund boards underperform relative to those located in states with few political trustees on public pension fund boards (see Internet Appendix Table IA.XXXII). Our finding that other institutional investors perform no worse in states with heavy political representation on public pension fund boards indicates that the pool of potential public appointees in these states is just as good as in states without political trustees.
Much of the evidence presented thus far is consistent with the Control channel, specifically, the tendency to overweight local investments in RE and VC and the underperformance of these investments. These results establish an important role for Control in explaining the underperformance of political boards, but the finding that pension funds with political boards invest more in smaller funds with fewer investors offered by less experienced GPs, and the fact that these funds tend to underperform, could be consistent with the other explanations as well. If politicians receive kickbacks or campaign contributions to invest in these funds, then such investments could be driven by Corruption. If they have fewer financial skills or less education than other trustees, then the selection of these funds could reflect Confusion.
article.V. Political Contributions and Performan:
In this section, we examine the extent to which the results are driven by varying political engagement across types of board members (theCorruptionchannel). Specifically, we examine the relationship between performance and political contributions to the campaigns of elected officials, especially from the finance industry. For this analysis, we obtain contributions data for board members of the 46 largest pension funds in our sample over the period 1999 to 2011 from the Follow the Money database.42The political contribution variables capture contributions to board members who participate in political electionsduringtheir tenure as a board member. We thus match a given individual's tenure as a board member to simultaneous involvement in politics to more precisely capture potential political incentives and agency conflicts.
We use political contributions from the finance industry to construct two proxies for opportunistic incentives. First,FinanceContrib/LP sizemeasures contributions from the finance industry relative to the LP's AUM. When estimating this ratio, contributions from the finance industry are expressed in millions of dollars and the LP's AUM are expressed in billions of dollars. Second,%Finance Contributionsmeasures political contributions received from institutions in the finance industry as a percentage of total contributions. We also control for the natural logarithm of total contributions.
Table10reports the results of specifications that include the trustee categories and the contributions variables. Columns (1) and (5) replicate the baseline models from Table3for this subsample. Overall, our proxies for political incentives appear to be negatively related to performance. Pension funds managed by trustees who receive relatively more contributions from the financial industry have lower returns. The ratio of finance contributions to LP size enters significantly and robustly in both specifications. Its magnitude in the second column suggests that, for an additional $100,000 of financial contributions, a $10 billion pension fund would have 0.28 percentage points worse net IRR and 1.16% lower multiple of invested capital. Additionally, contributions received from the finance industry as a percentage of total contributions are significantly negatively related to multiple of invested capital, with an additional percentage point coming from the finance industry associated with a 0.31% reduction in multiple of invested capital.
The vast majority of trustees who receive political contributions comprise state board members, and controlling for political contributions explains part of their underperformance. While inclusion of the contribution variables does not completely eliminate the state-appointed and state-ex officio effects, their significance indicates that at least some of the underperformance of elected officials relates to political incentives. To the extent that politicians derive personal gain from political contributions, these results lend some support to the Corruption channel. However, we note that, given the quality of the proxies available, it is difficult to fully distinguish between the Control and Corruption channels.
Importantly, our political contributions proxies capture only one of the channels through which poor incentives can affect performance. Other channels may also be at play. For example, many state-appointed and state-ex officio trustees are appointed to their position by another government official who serves in a higher position in the hierarchy. We do not capture the incentives of such persons. Another potential channel is that a financial institution provides contributions to the political party and the political party donates to the candidate who is a board member.
article.VI. Experience, Skills, and Performance:
Our final set of tests examines the extent to which differences in performance are driven by varying financial expertise and experience across types of board members (the Confusion channel). For this analysis, we obtain biographical data on the board members of the 46 largest pension funds in our sample. In Table2, we see that different categories of trustees have various backgrounds. When analyzing the role of board member skills and characteristics, we focus on trustee's prior professional experience. In particular, we capture the percentage of trustees with priorAsset Management,Financial, andRelatedprofessional experience. Additionally, we control for the percentage of board members with prior executive experience in the private sector, the percentage of pension fund trustees who are union members, and the percentage of trustees with a relevant educational degree.
Columns (1) and (4) of Table11replicate the baseline models from Table3for this subsample, and find similar magnitudes and statistical significance for the coefficients on the board composition variables. In columns (2) and (5), we find that pension funds governed by a higher percentage of trustees with asset management, financial, or related experience obtain higher returns on their PE investments. The coefficients on the three variables are highly significant, indicating that adding more board members with relevant prior experience is positively related to performance. Comparing columns (1) and (2), we see that the negative performance of participant-elected trustees is fully explained by a financial experience effect. Columns (3) and (6) show that adding political contribution controls does not materially attenuate the result—both experience and contribution variables remain significant.
To illustrate the magnitude of the coefficients in Table11, consider two similar pension funds governed by a five-member board with the same composition of trustees. Fund A has two board members with prior asset management experience, whereas Fund B has only one board member with such experience. Based on columns (2) and (5), Fund A will select PE investments that deliver 1.59 (=1/5 × 7.967) percentage points higher net IRR and 5.46% (=1/5 × 0.273) higher multiple of capital than the investments selected by Fund B.
We discuss only the coefficients on the variables measuring relevant prior professional experience because our estimates indicate that these are the most important characteristics. In Internet Appendix Table IA.XXXIV, we further compare the importance of relevant prior executive, union, and education experience, and show that these variables are not significantly related to performance after controlling for past professional experience.
We conclude that lower financial skills can explain almost all of the underperformance of participant-elected trustees, but do not explain the lower returns of state-appointed and state-ex officio trustees. Indeed, the coefficients on these state trustee groups are, if anything,largerin column (2) of Table11than in column (1). Based on the summary statistics, the main reason for this is likely due to state-appointed and state-ex officio trustees having relatively high prior financial skills and experience. Thus, we find no support for the Confusion channel in explaining the underperformance of politically dominated boards. Interestingly, controlling for financial skills and experience increases the coefficients on underperformance for public-appointed board members. Put differently, controlling for their higher level of financial experience, we find considerable unexplained underperformance for boards with a greater fraction of public-appointed members. This finding is surprising, as it suggests that public-appointed board members may face other incentives, perhaps related to the fact that they themselves are financial practitioners. We leave exploration of this puzzle to future research.
article.VII. Conclusion:
In this paper, we examine the effect of political representatives on governance and decision making in public organizations, and the channels through which political influence operates. We exploit variation in board composition across public pension funds and examine their performance in PE investments. We find that pension funds governed by boards heavily populated by state-appointed, state-ex officio, and participant-elected trustees invest in PE funds that deliver lower net IRR and multiple of invested capital.
Our findings provide suggestive evidence in support of two potential channels behind the underperformance of boards of trustees that are heavily populated by politicians. In exploring the first channel, Control, we find three failures that explain approximately half of the underperformance of pension funds with boards heavy in state-appointed and state-ex officio members. First, such pension funds invest more in RE, a category that delivers lower returns. Second, these funds overweight local in-state investments in RE and VC, which negatively affects performance. And third, poorly governed pension funds are more likely to invest in small funds that have few other investors and are managed by inexperienced GPs. These results lend broad support for the Control channel, whereby political representatives direct investment into PE funds that may be perceived as supporting state economic development but do not maximize expected returns subject to a given level of risk or minimize risk subject to a given level of expected returns.
When we explore the second channel, Corruption, we find that part of the underperformance by state officials can be explained by political contributions received from the finance industry relative to the pension fund's AUM. We document that pension funds governed by board members who receive relatively more contributions from the finance industry realize lower returns. To the extent that politicians derive personal gains from political contributions, these findings suggest a possible role for the Corruption channel. However, we note that, given the quality of the proxies available to explore this channel, it is difficult to fully disentangle the Corruption and Control channels, and this room remains for alternative interpretations of our various findings that may emphasize the different channels to a different degree.
In contrast, when we exploit variation in board members’ professional experience across pension funds, we find no support for a Confusion channel whereby a lack of knowledge, skills, or experience would drive the underperformance of politically dominated boards. While prior asset management, financial, or related experience is valuable and associated with the selection of PE funds that deliver higher performance, state-appointed and state-ex officio trustees generally score well on these experience measures. Low prior financial experience explains the poor performance of boards with a high proportion of participant-elected board members, but does not explain the underperformance of state-appointed and state-ex officio trustees.
In some ways, our results on the impact of politicians on public asset management boards contrast with the literature that studies the effects of politicians on corporate boards. The corporate literature finds that when politicians serve on boards, they bring benefits to firm shareholders, either through enhanced access to procurement contracts or a lower cost of capital, which helps the board in its primary objective of creating shareholder value. In the public pension context, the presence of politicians on boards appears to work against pension funds’ primary objective of delivering the benefits promised to participants as efficiently as possible for taxpayers. In other ways, however, the two sets of results can be viewed as consistent, as both benefits to firms with political board representation and costs to public pension systems with political board representation may be paid for to a large extent by taxpayers.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
